races.name: Paris-Roubaix Hauts-de-France
races.description: The 123rd edition of the Queen of the Classics — cycling's Hell of the North. Starts in Compiègne, neutralised roll-out to the official start with racing from ~11:05 CEST, heading northeast through the Hauts-de-France region for 258.3km across 30 cobbled sectors (54.8km total pavé). Key sectors: Quiévy à Fontaine au Tertre (4★, 3.7km, sector 28), Haveluy à Wallers (4★, 2.5km, sector 20 — new for 2026), Trouée d'Arenberg (5★, 2.3km, sector 19 — 95.3km from finish), Mons-en-Pévèle (5★, 3.0km, sector 7 — ~49km to go), Carrefour de l'Arbre (5★, 2.1km, sector 3 — ~17km to go), before a short Roubaix cobble sector into the velodrome. Records at stake: Van der Poel chasing a record-equalling 4th win (alongside De Vlaeminck and Boonen); Pogačar chasing his 5th Monument to join Merckx, De Vlaeminck and Van Looy as only the 4th rider to win all five. History note: Van der Poel has won the last 3 editions solo (2023, 2024, 2025) — the first rider to do so since Francesco Moser's 3 consecutive in 1978-80.
races.rider_notes: Van der Poel (Alpecin-Premier Tech) is 3-time defending champion and bookmakers' favourite. Won all three previous editions solo. Pogačar (UAE) won Milan-San Remo and Tour of Flanders already this spring — is hunting the Monument Grand Slam (5/5). In 2025 he was alongside Van der Poel before crashing with 38km to go; returns determined not to repeat. Van Aert (Visma) is Flanders-heartland's most desperate Roubaix dream. Ganna (INEOS) skipped Itzulia specifically to prepare for this. Evenepoel not racing. Reusser and Le Court-Pienaar out (women's field). Philipsen (Alpecin) finished 2nd in 2023 and 2024 — is he due a win without needing to lead out Van der Poel? Pedersen is 2x podium finisher.

broadcasts.notes: Free-to-air on France TV and L'Équipe from race start.
Free-to-air on Sporza / VRT from race start.
Free-to-air on NOS from race start.
Live from race start on TNT Sports and HBO Max. Plans from £25.99/month.
Peacock and NBC Sports from 4:30 AM ET (08:30 UTC). ASO races are exclusive to Peacock/NBC in the US. Race coverage includes both men's and women's.
Free live coverage on SBS On Demand from race start.
Eurosport and HBO Max / Discovery+ across Europe from race start.

data_confidence.notes: 123rd edition confirmed. Distance 258.3km confirmed by PCS LiveStats and multiple sources. Men's neutral start at 10:50 CEST per BikeRadar official info. Racing start 11:05 CEST per PCS. Finish expected 16:35-17:05 CEST. 30 cobbled sectors covering 54.8km confirmed by Cyclist and others. 5-star sectors: Trouée d'Arenberg, Mons-en-Pévèle, Carrefour de l'Arbre. New for 2026: Haveluy à Wallers (4★). UK on TNT Sports/HBO Max confirmed (ASO race). US on Peacock/NBC confirmed (ASO race). Canada on FloBikes confirmed. Australia SBS free confirmed. Evenepoel confirmed not racing. Van der Poel seeking record-equalling 4th win. Pogačar seeking 5th Monument.

races.name: Paris-Roubaix Femmes Hauts-de-France
races.description: The 6th edition of Paris-Roubaix Femmes — for the first time held on the same day as the men's race (previously a standalone Saturday event; this is a one-year arrangement). 143.1km from Denain to the Roubaix Velodrome across 20 cobbled sectors totalling 33.7km (3 new sectors added for 2026, +4.5km vs 2025, including the 4★ Haveluy à Wallers sector). The route wastes no time: first pavé hits at 29.3km (Solesmes à Haussy, 2★). Key decisive sectors in the finale: Mons-en-Pévèle (5★, ~48km to go), Carrefour de l'Arbre (5★, ~17km to go), then the short Roubaix cobble sector into the velodrome for a 1.5-lap finish. Note: TV coverage controversy — broadcasts will only show the final 50-60km (after the men's race finishes), cutting most of the early cobbled action. No rider has won this race twice. Defending champion: Pauline Ferrand-Prévot (Visma-Lease a Bike, 2025 — last-minute entry defending her title).
races.broadcast_note: ⚠️ TV coverage ONLY from final ~50-60km (after men's race finishes). Women's race starts 14:45 CEST but broadcast starts after ~17:00 CEST. UK on HBO Max shows women's footage after men's finish.
races.rider_notes: Kopecky (SD Worx-Protime, 2024 winner) is the clear top favourite — best combined climbing/cobbles/sprint package and is backed by Wiebes as a devastating second option. Ferrand-Prévot (Visma-Lease a Bike) defended her 2025 title in a last-minute programme change (originally not on her schedule). No rider has won this race twice — she has the chance to be first. Wiebes is the world's fastest sprinter; her ability to survive the cobbles is the question. Longo Borghini (UAE, 2022 winner) is a proven Roubaix specialist. Vos (Visma) was 4th in 2025. Jackson (St Michel-Preference Home, 2023 winner) is a shock-edition specialist. Absent: Reusser and Le Court-Pienaar (fractures from Tour of Flanders); Ferrand-Prévot originally not planned.

broadcasts.notes: Free-to-air in France. Broadcast starts after men's race finishes (~17:00 CEST / 14:55 UTC), showing final 50-60km.
Free on Sporza/VRT, from ~17:00 CEST after men's race finishes.
Live footage shown after men's race finishes — approximately from 17:00 CEST (15:00 UTC / 16:00 BST) showing final ~50-60km of the women's race. Plans from £25.99/month.
Women's coverage from ~10:55 AM ET (14:55 UTC), after men's race finishes. Shows final ~50-60km.
Women's coverage from ~10:55 AM ET. CAN$39.99/month.
Free on SBS On Demand from when coverage begins (after men's race).
Eurosport and HBO Max after men's race finishes.

data_confidence.notes: 6th edition (2021 inaugural; NBC Sports erroneously says 5th). Distance 143.1km, Denain → Roubaix confirmed. 20 cobbled sectors, 33.7km of pavé confirmed by Cyclist and others. Start time 14:45 CEST (12:45 UTC) confirmed by IDL Pro Cycling preview ('Start: 2:45 p.m.'). Finish ~18:20 CEST per IDL ('approximately 6:20 p.m.'). 3 new sectors added vs 2025 including 4★ Haveluy à Wallers (sector 20). TV broadcast controversy confirmed: Cyclingnews reports 'women's race footage set to start only after the men's race finishes, showing around 50-60km.' Kopecky top favourite (2024 winner, backed by Wiebes). Ferrand-Prévot defending (last-minute entry confirmed). Reusser/Le Court-Pienaar absent (Tour of Flanders fractures). Marianne Vos racing with Visma.

recent_results.race: Itzulia Basque Country Stage 6 (WT) – Goizper-Antzuola → Bergara
recent_results.notes: Rain-affected final stage. Seixas won the overall Itzulia Basque Country — the 65th edition — in dominant fashion with 3 stage wins (ITT, Stage 2 mountain solo, Stage 5 mountains win) and the biggest GC gap in modern Itzulia history. A landmark triumph for French cycling from a 19-year-old.

recent_results.race: Région Pays de la Loire Tour Stage 4 (Pro) – Brûlon → Le Mans
recent_results.notes: Final stage, Le Mans city circuit with Côte de Gazonfier.
Merlier's 3rd consecutive Scheldeprijs. Returned from knee injury (only 2nd race of 2026). Groenewegen crashed in final 10km; Philipsen boxed in.
Kool finally broke her run of 3x runner-up finishes at Scheldeprijs. Crash-marred finale.
Pogačar's record-equalling 3rd Flanders win. Monument #12. Attacked final Kwaremont with 18km to go. Van der Poel chasing, Evenepoel impressive Monument debut.

notes: Paul Seixas, 19, won Itzulia Basque Country overall — 3 stage wins (ITT, Stage 2, Stage 5) and the biggest winning margin in recent race history. A historic debut stage race triumph.

Tadej Pogačar has won 3 of his first 3 races in 2026: Strade Bianche, Milan-San Remo, Tour of Flanders. A win today would make it 4/4 and complete the Monument Grand Slam — only the 4th rider ever (alongside Merckx, De Vlaeminck, Van Looy).
Mathieu van der Poel seeks a record-equalling 4th Paris-Roubaix win (alongside De Vlaeminck 1972/74/75/77 and Boonen 2005/08/09/12). He has won 3 consecutively (2023, 2024, 2025).
Wout van Aert is chasing his dream race. Has been close multiple times (2nd 2022, 3rd 2023) but luck has not favoured him.

Women's Paris-Roubaix is on the same day as men's for the first time in 2026. TV coverage of women's race is severely curtailed — only final 50-60km shown on broadcast. Confirmed to revert to its own day in 2027.

Absent from Paris-Roubaix men: Remco Evenepoel (chose not to start). Absent from Paris-Roubaix Femmes: Marlen Reusser and Kim Le Court-Pienaar (fractures from Tour of Flanders). Marianne Vos and Pauline Ferrand-Prévot both racing (Ferrand-Prévot was a late addition).
Weather forecast: temperatures dropping to ~11°C on Sunday. Dry expected but cool.
Filippo Ganna (INEOS) skipped Itzulia specifically to prepare for Paris-Roubaix.

upcoming.notes: Start of the Ardennes Classics week. Van der Poel and Pogačar move to a very different profile.

upcoming.race: Amstel Gold Race Ladies Edition (WWT, Netherlands)

upcoming.notes: The Mur de Huy specialist race.
La Doyenne — the final Monument of the spring Classics season.

issues: Women's race edition: NBC Sports says '5th edition'; Yahoo Sports says '6th time'. 2021 was the inaugural edition, so 2026 = 6th. Using 6th.

Women's start time: IDL Pro Cycling preview confirms '2:45 p.m.' = 14:45 CEST / 12:45 UTC. Finish ~18:20 CEST. US coverage starts at ~10:55 AM ET (14:55 UTC) = approximately 2h10 into the women's race, confirming this aligns with showing the final ~50-60km.
Men's start: BikeRadar confirms 10:50 CEST official start. PCS LiveStats shows 11:05 CEST (this is the racing start after neutralised rollout from Compiègne). Using 10:50 CEST as start_time_local.

Women's broadcast: Cyclingnews explicitly confirms TV coverage starts only after men's race finishes (~17:00 CEST), showing approximately the final 50-60km only. This has been noted as a 'massive affront to women's cycling' by some commentators and race director has confirmed the women's race will have its own day from 2027.

Itzulia final result: Cyclingnews confirms 'Paul Seixas clinches first WorldTour stage race title' and 'AJ August wins rain-battered final stage'. Full final GC gap not retrieved but Seixas was leading by ~1:59 with two stages remaining.
